summary: Research-grounded design swarm for ai-crucible Epic 4 (graduation/saturation/differential/persistence)

research.summary: The literature settles a clean three-state promotion rule. Keep the existing Wilson-band gate on Claude's own solve-rate (it already rules out trivial AND impossible), then add cross-family validation as a SEPARATE, abstention-aware gate — and require BOTH a cross-family SOLVE condition and a cross-family JUDGE condition, because living benchmarks show they fail differently: SWE-bench Verified human screening flagged 38.3% of items as underspecified and 61.1% as having broken tests (a fairness problem the solve-rate cannot see), while BBH defines "graduated" purely by below-human solvability (a non-triviality problem the fairness vote cannot see). The honest handling of the 2-seat sub-quorum panel is given directly by Jung/Brahman/Choi's "Trust or Escalate" (ICLR 2025) and the classical selective-prediction line (Chow 1970; Geifman–El-Yaniv 2017): a low-confidence jury must ABSTAIN (return DEFER → escalate to the Claude Designer gold check), never emit fake confidence. This maps one-to-one onto the codebase's existing SeatedPanel.meets_quorum / .escalate and the §11.1 path, so no new machinery is needed — only a graduation predicate that consumes those signals.

findings.claim: Living benchmarks treat an item as high-trust only after human filtering removes items that are mis-specified or have broken graders — fairness/validity, not just difficulty, is a graduation criterion, and the two failure modes are large and distinct.
findings.paper_title: Introducing SWE-bench Verified
findings.authors: OpenAI (Neil Chowdhury, James Aung, et al.) with the SWE-bench authors
findings.key_finding: 93 professional developers screened SWE-bench; 38.3% of samples were flagged for underspecified problem statements and 61.1% for unit tests that may unfairly fail valid solutions, filtering out 68.3% of the original set to yield 500 'Verified' items.
findings.design_implication: Graduation must have a cross-family JUDGE-FAIRNESS condition that is SEPARATE from solve-rate: the panel rates whether the puzzle is well-specified and the oracle/grader is not broken. A puzzle can sit perfectly in Claude's Wilson band yet be an unfair/broken item. Implement panel_fairness_ok as a distinct clause in the promotion predicate, not folded into solve evidence.

findings.claim: The canonical definition of a 'graduated/hard' benchmark item is one that is genuinely non-trivial relative to a strong external baseline — i.e. cross-population SOLVE evidence, not single-model difficulty.
findings.paper_title: Challenging BIG-Bench Tasks and Whether Chain-of-Thought Can Solve Them (BIG-Bench Hard)
findings.authors: Mirac Suzgun, Nathan Scales, Nathanael Schärli, et al.
findings.key_finding: BBH is constructed as the 23 BIG-Bench tasks where the best prior model did NOT beat the average human rater; tasks the best model already beat humans on were pruned out (live-prune to keep only genuinely hard items).
findings.design_implication: Add a cross-family SOLVE condition: a puzzle graduates only if it is non-trivial for OTHER families too (the panel/CohortSolver cross-family solve-rate is itself inside a discriminating band, e.g. not 100%). This also directly powers the differential typology — the same cross-family solve-rate that gates graduation classifies the puzzle (Claude-fails-others-pass vs everyone-fails vs Claude-strength). One measurement, two uses.

findings.claim: When a judge/jury is not confident enough, the correct action is to ABSTAIN and escalate, with a PROVABLE human-agreement guarantee on the non-abstained subset — even weak judges become trustworthy via selective evaluation rather than via more seats.
findings.paper_title: Trust or Escalate: LLM Judges with Provable Guarantees for Human Agreement
findings.authors: Jaehun Jung, Faeze Brahman, Yejin Choi
findings.key_finding: Cascaded Selective Evaluation lets cheaper judges rule only when confident and escalate otherwise; this provably guarantees a user-specified human-agreement level on covered cases — even Mistral-7B reaches >80% human agreement at ~80% coverage.
findings.design_implication: This is the exact license for the sub-quorum design. The cross-family JUDGE clause is THREE-valued: PASS (confident fair), FAIL (confident unfair), DEFER (low confidence). DEFER fires when SeatedPanel.meets_quorum is False (2<3 PoLL) OR reliability_weighted_vote returns escalate=True (thin margin). DEFER routes to the Claude Designer gold check (§11.1) — promotion under DEFER requires an explicit attested Designer override, never an auto-pass. The 2-seat panel still validly graduates the puzzles it IS confident about; it only abstains on the uncertain ones.

findings.claim: A diverse panel reduces intra-model bias precisely because its judges span disjoint model families; the value of cross-family judging is structural, which is why a sub-quorum (2 of 3) panel is genuinely weaker and must be disclosed rather than treated as full strength.
findings.paper_title: Replacing Judges with Juries: Evaluating LLM Generations with a Panel of Diverse Models (PoLL)
findings.authors: Pat Verga, Sebastian Hofstätter, Sophia Althammer, et al.
findings.key_finding: A Panel of LLM evaluators composed of disjoint model families correlates with human judgment better than a single large judge while exhibiting less intra-model bias and costing ~1/7 as much.
findings.design_implication: Justifies REQUIRING the cross-family condition at all (Claude's own band cannot self-certify fairness — self-preference bias). It also justifies the abstention default: with only 2 disjoint families < PoLL's 3, the bias-reduction guarantee is not met, so the predicate must DEFER on judge fairness rather than claim panel-grade confidence. Keep meets_quorum as the hard switch into the deferral branch.

findings.claim: Selective prediction (classification with a reject option) is the principled way to gate a decision on confidence: abstain when expected risk exceeds the cost of deferral, trading coverage for guaranteed risk.
findings.paper_title: On Optimum Recognition Error and Reject Tradeoff (foundational reject option)
findings.key_finding: The optimal rule abstains exactly when the conditional expected risk of predicting exceeds a fixed reject cost, formalizing the accuracy-vs-coverage tradeoff that all selective prediction inherits.
findings.design_implication: Frames the promotion gate as a reject-option decision: do not promote on a low-confidence cross-family verdict; defer. The 'reject cost' here is the cost of escalating to the Designer — cheap relative to the cost of admitting an unfair/trivial puzzle into Arena. Set the panel-confidence threshold (escalate_below margin) as the explicit, recorded reject threshold in the predicate config (PIN_PER_STEP).

findings.claim: Selective classification can deliver a GUARANTEED risk bound at a chosen coverage by abstaining below a confidence threshold — directly applicable to bounding the rate of wrongly-graduated puzzles when the jury is weak.
findings.paper_title: Selective Classification for Deep Neural Networks
findings.key_finding: The SGR algorithm selects a confidence threshold (via guaranteed-risk bound) so the selective risk stays under a user-specified level with high probability, at the cost of reduced coverage.
findings.design_implication: Gives a numeric handle: rather than a hand-picked margin, the deferral threshold for the cross-family judge clause can be tied to a target false-graduation risk, reusing the already-present conformal_coverage_interval primitive to report what coverage/risk the chosen N and threshold actually buy. Report (covered / deferred / risk) as a graduation receipt, not just a boolean.

findings.claim: Learning-to-defer formalizes a system that either decides or routes the decision to a human expert; deferral is a first-class, consistent action, not a failure state — supporting an explicit DEFER outcome with an owner rather than a silent fallthrough.
findings.paper_title: Consistent Estimators for Learning to Defer to an Expert
findings.key_finding: They give the first Bayes-consistent surrogate loss for jointly learning a predictor and a rejector that defers to a downstream human expert, treating 'defer to expert' as a principled third option.
findings.design_implication: Model the predicate's return as an explicit enum PROMOTE | HOLD | DEFER_TO_DESIGNER rather than a bool. DEFER_TO_DESIGNER names the Claude Designer as the expert owner (UNCERTAINTY_GATED_HUMANS), writes a deferral event to the hash-chained attestation store, and blocks auto-promotion until an attested override resolves it. This keeps the sub-quorum path honest and auditable.

findings.claim: Dynamic/living benchmarks decide an item is high-value when it fools current strong models but not a competent external solver — i.e. graduation is defined by a model-vs-external-baseline gap, validating the differential framing as the graduation signal.
findings.paper_title: Dynabench: Rethinking Benchmarking in NLP
findings.authors: Douwe Kiela, Max Bartolo, Yixin Nie, et al.
findings.key_finding: Dynabench keeps examples that fool a state-of-the-art model but not a human, using the human-vs-model gap as the criterion for an example's diagnostic value.
findings.design_implication: Confirms that the cross-family SOLVE condition should be relational (Claude-rate vs other-family-rate), not absolute. A puzzle where Claude fails but cross-family solvers pass is the highest-value Arena item; the predicate should record this differential class at graduation time so the Arena entry is born with its typology label, feeding Epic 4's differential typology directly.

research.recommendation:
Implement Lab→Arena promotion as a three-valued predicate that keeps the Wilson band on Claude's own rate and adds TWO abstention-aware cross-family clauses. Concrete signature and predicate:

promote_decision(claude_successes, claude_n, cohort_successes, cohort_n, panel_score) -> Decision, where Decision ∈ {PROMOTE, HOLD, DEFER_TO_DESIGNER}.

Clause 1 — DISCRIMINATING (unchanged): claude_band_ok = graduates(claude_successes, claude_n)  # 0.10 ≤ wilson_lower AND wilson_upper ≤ 0.90 on Claude's own rate. If this fails → HOLD (puzzle stays in Lab; it is trivial or impossible for Claude).

Clause 2 — CROSS-FAMILY SOLVE (new, non-trivial-for-others): cohort_nontrivial = wilson_upper(cohort_successes, cohort_n) ≤ 0.90  (the cross-family panel/CohortSolver does NOT trivially ace it). Use the existing Wilson upper bound on the cohort solve-rate; record the differential class (Claude-fails-others-pass / everyone-fails / Claude-strength) from the two rates at the same time so the Arena entry is born labeled. If cohort evidence shows the puzzle is trivial for other families → HOLD.

Clause 3 — CROSS-FAMILY JUDGE FAIRNESS (new, abstention-aware, the keystone): read the composed panel's signals that ALREADY EXIST. If panel.meets_quorum is False (2 < PoLL 3) OR panel.score.metadata['escalate'] is True (reliability_weighted_vote margin ≤ escalate_below) → the fairness verdict is LOW-CONFIDENCE → return DEFER_TO_DESIGNER. Do not pass, do not fail. Otherwise, if the confident panel verdict is 'unfair/broken' → HOLD; if 'fair' → fairness_ok = True.

Compose: PROMOTE iff claude_band_ok AND cohort_nontrivial AND fairness_ok AND NOT deferred. Any low-confidence fairness signal ⇒ DEFER_TO_DESIGNER (the §11.1 Trust-or-Escalate path). Promotion out of a DEFER state is allowed ONLY via an explicit Designer gold-check override that is written to the append-only hash-chained attestation store (event: graduation_deferred → graduation_override, with the panel margin, meets_quorum=false, seat ids, and the differential class recorded). Never auto-promote on a deferred fairness verdict and never synthesize a fake quorum.

This requires no new dependency and no new aggregation machinery: it consumes SeatedPanel.meets_quorum / .escalate, reuse stats.graduates and stats.wilson_interval for both rates, and writes through the existing attestation.py event store. Tie the escalate_below margin and (optionally) the cohort threshold to a recorded target false-graduation risk using the already-present conformal_coverage_interval, and emit a graduation receipt (covered / held / deferred + the risk/coverage the chosen N buys) rather than a bare boolean — selective-prediction discipline (Chow 1970; Geifman–El-Yaniv 2017) made auditable.

Direction on the 'solve vs judge' question: gate on BOTH. SWE-bench Verified proves judge-fairness catches a failure mode (38.3% underspecified, 61.1% broken graders) that solve-rate is blind to; BBH/Dynabench prove cross-family solve-evidence catches the non-triviality failure mode that a fairness vote is blind to. They are orthogonal and both required. The sub-quorum honesty lives ENTIRELY in Clause 3's abstention — the SOLVE clauses are statistical (Wilson) and always computable, so the panel's weakness degrades gracefully to deferral on fairness only, while still graduating the puzzles the 2-seat panel IS confident about.
research.open_risks: 1) i.i.d. and N: cohort solve-rate Wilson bounds assume the cross-family cohort is run at the same N=20 floor; if the cohort is run at smaller N its Wilson upper bound will rarely drop below 0.90 and almost everything will look 'non-trivial,' weakening Clause 2 — fix the cohort N floor explicitly. 2) Confidence calibration of a 2-seat panel: Trust-or-Escalate's provable guarantee assumes a calibrated confidence signal (their Simulated Annotators); a raw reliability_weighted_vote margin is NOT a calibrated human-agreement probability, so DEFER thresholds chosen by margin are heuristic, not provably bounded — the conformal_coverage_interval receipt mitigates by reporting the spread but does not manufacture calibration. 3) Designer-override drift: routing DEFER to the Claude Designer reintroduces a same-family judge on the FAIRNESS axis (the exact self-preference EXTERNAL_VERIFIER guards against). This is acceptable ONLY because it is the disclosed, attested escalation of last resort under structural sub-quorum — but a high DEFER rate silently shifts the instrument's fairness authority back onto Claude. Monitor the deferral rate as an instrument-health metric; a rising DEFER fraction is the signal to recruit a third disjoint family, not to relax the threshold. 4) The omega/non-provisional-seat constraint is unchanged: this predicate makes the sub-quorum panel USABLE and honest, but it does not make the seats non-provisional — that still needs ≥3 independent human annotators, which remains on ice by structural constraint.

research.summary: Saturation must NOT be a single-snapshot test (wilson_upper > 0.90 on one batch flaps under noise). The defensible rule is a sustained-regime-change declaration on a per-(puzzle, model) binary stream, made anti-flapping by hysteresis (two thresholds + a dwell condition) — exactly the Schmitt-trigger / debouncing pattern, formalized by sequential change-detection (Page's CUSUM 1954, Wald's SPRT 1945) and made statistically honest under continuous monitoring by anytime-valid confidence sequences (Howard et al. 2021). The living-benchmark literature (LiveBench, Dynabench, the BBH live-prune, and the 2026 systematic saturation study) confirms the *policy*: declare a task to have outlived diagnostic value only when a *capable* model clears it consistently, demote-not-delete, and keep a guard band so a re-promotion never fires on a noisy run. The unit is per-(puzzle, model) for the *trigger* (which model saturated it) aggregated into a per-puzzle *Arena status* (a puzzle leaves Arena only when the FRONTIER, not one lucky seed, has caught up).

findings.claim: Demoting on a single observation that crosses a threshold is the textbook failure mode hysteresis exists to fix: a noisy stream near one threshold should cause only ONE state switch, and reversing requires crossing a SECOND, separated threshold. ai-crucible's demote/re-promote oscillation IS switch bounce.
findings.paper_title: Schmitt trigger (dual-threshold hysteresis for noise immunity and debouncing)
findings.authors: O. H. Schmitt (concept); standard digital-electronics canon
findings.key_finding: A two-threshold (UTP for rising, LTP for falling) comparator with a separating gap turns a noisy signal into a single clean transition; to toggle back the signal must cross the opposite threshold, eliminating chatter near the switch point.
findings.design_implication: Implement saturation with a HYSTERESIS BAND, not one cut. Demote Arena→Regression only when wilson_lower(saturation evidence) >= 0.90 (high bar to LEAVE); re-promote Regression→Arena only when wilson_upper <= 0.75 (must fall well BELOW the demotion line). The 0.75–0.90 dead band is the guard band; a single noisy run inside it changes nothing.

findings.claim: Declaring a sustained regime change from a noisy binary stream is the exact problem CUSUM was built for — accumulate evidence over the stream and signal only when the cumulative drift exceeds a decision boundary, which is far more robust to single-point noise than a per-batch threshold (Shewhart) chart.
findings.paper_title: Continuous Inspection Schemes (CUSUM)
findings.key_finding: The cumulative-sum chart detects small sustained shifts in a process mean with provably lower detection delay than per-sample (Shewhart) charts at a fixed false-alarm rate, because it integrates evidence rather than testing each point in isolation.
findings.design_implication: Pair the hysteresis band with a DWELL CONDITION sourced from accumulated evidence: require the saturation signal to hold across >=2 consecutive independent runs AND a minimum accumulated N (e.g. 3*min_k attempts post-trigger) before demotion fires. This is CUSUM's 'integrate before you signal' applied to the run-to-run solve stream, killing single-batch false alarms.

findings.claim: The catalog monitors each puzzle continuously across many runs and demotes at a data-dependent stopping time ('when it looks saturated'). A fixed-N Wilson interval is only valid at ONE pre-chosen N; peeking across runs and acting when it crosses inflates the false-demotion rate. SPRT bounds Type-I/Type-II error for exactly this sequential, optional-stopping setting.
findings.paper_title: Sequential Tests of Statistical Hypotheses (SPRT)
findings.key_finding: The sequential probability ratio test accepts H1 when the likelihood ratio exceeds A=(1-beta)/alpha and H0 when it falls below B=beta/(1-alpha), guaranteeing Type-I<=alpha and Type-II<=beta at the (random) stopping time while minimizing expected sample size.
findings.design_implication: Frame saturation as a one-sided sequential test of H0: p<=0.90 (still diagnostic) vs H1: p>=0.97 (saturated), with alpha=beta=0.05. Demote only when the accumulated log-likelihood ratio crosses the A boundary. This makes 'demote when it looks solved across runs' a calibrated decision, not a peeking artifact — and naturally yields the dwell (it needs enough consistent successes to cross A).

findings.claim: If the team prefers to keep reporting Wilson-style intervals while monitoring every run (the current code style), the fixed-N Wilson must be swapped for a time-uniform confidence SEQUENCE, which is valid simultaneously at every run/sample count — the rigorous fix for 'I checked the interval after each batch and acted on it.'
findings.paper_title: Time-uniform, nonparametric, nonasymptotic confidence sequences
findings.authors: Steven R. Howard, Aaditya Ramdas, Jon McAuliffe, Jasjeet Sekhon
findings.key_finding: Confidence sequences built from nonnegative supermartingales + Ville's inequality maintain their coverage guarantee uniformly over all sample sizes, so an analyst may continuously monitor and stop at any data-dependent time without inflating error.
findings.design_implication: For the demotion predicate use a time-uniform LOWER confidence bound on the solve rate (a confidence-sequence lower bound, slightly wider than fixed-N Wilson) rather than re-reading wilson_lower(successes, n) after every run. Reuse the existing Bayesian beta-binomial machinery in scoring/stats.py as the e-process/mixture-martingale backbone (a beta mixture is a valid e-value family), so this stays pure-Python with no new heavy dep.

findings.claim: Living benchmarks already operationalize 'a task outlived its diagnostic value': retire the OLDEST and EASIEST items on a cadence, replacing them with harder variants — empirically the maintainable saturation policy. This validates demote-not-delete + a frontier-relative (not absolute) saturation trigger.
findings.paper_title: LiveBench: A Challenging, Contamination-Free LLM Benchmark
findings.authors: White, Dooley, Roberts, Pal, ... Goldblum, Schwarzschild, Goldstein, et al.
findings.key_finding: LiveBench refreshes monthly, rotating out roughly one-sixth of items each cycle by prioritizing the oldest and currently-easiest questions, sustaining a ~1.2% median-score drop per update so the benchmark keeps separating frontier models.
findings.design_implication: Make the saturation trigger FRONTIER-RELATIVE and event-driven, not a standing cron: when a newly-admitted strong model (the §2 'Opus 4.8 ships' event) clears an Arena puzzle past the demotion bar, queue it for demotion review and spawn a fresh Lab cycle. Tag each demoted record with the model + date that saturated it, building the capability-evolution timeline the spec wants.

findings.claim: Adversarial/dynamic benchmarks (Dynabench) avoid the ceiling by tying difficulty to current model capability; the explicit lifecycle recommendation is that benchmarks carry retirement criteria that fire only when saturation indices CONSISTENTLY exceed high thresholds — i.e. sustained, not one reading.
findings.paper_title: Dynabench: Rethinking Benchmarking in NLP
findings.authors: Douwe Kiela, Max Bartolo, Yixin Nie, ... Adina Williams, et al.
findings.key_finding: Human-and-model-in-the-loop adversarial data collection keeps benchmark difficulty scaling with model capability, preventing the ceiling effect that retires static benchmarks; difficulty is defined relative to the current frontier, not an absolute score.
findings.design_implication: Define saturation relative to the de-saturated cross-family panel + the current model, never absolutely. A puzzle is 'saturated' only when a capable subject clears it consistently; everyone-fails stays in Arena (it is the §4 LLM-general gap, still maximally diagnostic). Gate the demotion predicate on model-capability class so a weak local model passing does NOT count as frontier saturation.

findings.claim: A 2026 systematic study of benchmark saturation makes the retirement criterion explicit: incorporate revision/retirement once saturation indices CONSISTENTLY exceed high thresholds, and notes a minority of well-designed benchmarks resist saturation — so consistency-over-time, not a single high score, is the defensible declaration of lost diagnostic value.
findings.paper_title: When AI Benchmarks Plateau: A Systematic Study of Benchmark Saturation
findings.key_finding: Across popular benchmarks, saturation should trigger lifecycle action only when saturation indices consistently exceed high thresholds over time; a high score in a single evaluation window is insufficient evidence of substantive task mastery.
findings.design_implication: Codify 'consistently exceed' as the dwell-K parameter: demotion requires the saturation signal to persist across >=2 independent runs spanning the hysteresis high bar. Expose this as a per-puzzle min_k-style config (the spec's 'configurable floor K') so high-value puzzles can demand a longer dwell before they leave Arena.

findings.claim: Anytime-valid inference (e-values / e-processes) is the modern, optional-stopping-safe replacement for the SPRT and gives the cleanest implementable primitive: a running statistic whose null expectation is <=1, so thresholding it at 1/alpha controls error at ANY stopping time — ideal for a catalog that decides 'demote now?' after each run.
findings.paper_title: Anytime validity is free: inducing sequential tests (and the e-value/e-process framework)
findings.authors: Aaditya Ramdas, Tudor Manole, et al. (game-theoretic / anytime-valid inference line)
findings.key_finding: An e-value has expectation <=1 under the null, so by Ville's inequality rejecting when it exceeds 1/alpha yields a valid test at any data-dependent stopping time; e-processes let one continuously monitor and stop whenever evidence is sufficient without alpha-inflation.
findings.design_implication: Implement the demotion trigger as a stored per-(puzzle,model) e-process: each run multiplies in a beta-binomial likelihood-ratio factor toward H1 (saturated). Demote when the e-process crosses 1/alpha (alpha=0.05 -> 20). This is one accumulating float per (puzzle,model), append-friendly to the existing hash-chained event store, pure-Python, and makes the dwell condition emergent rather than a hand-tuned magic number.

research.recommendation:
Saturation is NOT `wilson_upper > 0.90` on a single batch — that flaps. Implement a per-(puzzle, model) anti-flapping demotion predicate with three ANDed components, then aggregate to a per-puzzle Arena decision:

DEMOTE Arena -> Regression for (puzzle, model m) iff ALL of:
  (1) HYSTERESIS HIGH BAR: time-uniform lower confidence bound on m's solve rate >= 0.90
      (confidence-SEQUENCE lower bound per Howard 2021, NOT fixed-N wilson_lower — because we peek every run);
  (2) DWELL: the signal in (1) holds across >= dwell_K consecutive independent runs (default 2) AND
      accumulated post-trigger attempts >= 3*min_k (CUSUM 'integrate before signal'; the §2 consistency-over-time rule);
  (3) FRONTIER GATE: m is a current-frontier / panel-admitted capable subject (Dynabench relative-difficulty rule) —
      a weak local model passing never demotes; everyone-fails stays in Arena (it is the §4 LLM-general gap).

RE-PROMOTE Regression -> Arena iff the time-uniform UPPER bound on the best current subject's solve rate <= 0.75.
The 0.75 .. 0.90 dead band is the Schmitt-trigger guard band: a noisy run inside it changes nothing, killing demote/re-promote oscillation.

Cleanest implementation (matches repo style): store one accumulating per-(puzzle,model) E-PROCESS for H0: p<=0.90 vs H1: p>=0.97 (Wald SPRT framed as an anytime-valid e-value built on the beta-binomial machinery already in scoring/stats.py). Each `ai-crucible run` multiplies in the run's beta-binomial likelihood factor and appends the new e-value to the hash-chained attestation store. Demote when the e-process crosses 1/alpha = 20 (alpha=0.05) AND the frontier gate holds; this makes the dwell condition emergent (it cannot cross 20 on one lucky run) rather than a hand-tuned constant. Keep `min_k`/`dwell_K` per-puzzle configurable (the spec's open 'graduation-floor K').

UNIT: trigger is per-(puzzle, model) — record WHICH model + date saturated the puzzle (capability-evolution timeline). Arena MEMBERSHIP is per-puzzle-aggregate — a puzzle leaves Arena only when at least one frontier subject has crossed the demotion predicate; it stays in Arena as long as it discriminates ANY current frontier subject. Demote-not-delete, tag the saturating model, spawn a fresh Lab cycle (the LiveBench/Dynabench 'frontier caught up' event).
research.open_risks: 1) Independence: the e-process/SPRT error guarantees assume runs are i.i.d. at the puzzle's true p. Correlated failures (Liu 2025's 71% identical-failure recurrence) violate this within a batch — mitigate by treating each k-attempt RUN, not each attempt, as the e-process unit, and require dwell across SEPARATE runs (different seeds/dates). 2) min N before any demotion is allowed: the spec's N=20 floor is for graduation; demotion needs its own floor (recommend post-trigger accumulated N >= 3*min_k) or a 1/20-lucky-run could nudge the e-process. 3) Choosing H1 (p>=0.97) and the 0.75/0.90 band is a value judgment about 'how solved is solved' — these are policy knobs, not derived constants; document them and make per-puzzle configurable. 4) Frontier-gate definition depends on the panel admission bar, which is currently sub-quorum (2 seats) and provisional (omega on ice) — so 'is this model frontier-capable' inherits that disclosed weakness; until quorum, lean on the Claude Designer escalation already in place rather than auto-demoting on a 2-seat panel. 5) Re-promotion is rare-but-real (a regression in a new model version): the guard band handles noise, but a genuine capability REGRESSION should re-promote — make sure the upper-bound re-promotion path is actually wired, not just demotion. 6) The 2026 systematic-saturation arXiv id (2602.16763) and the exact e-value JRSS-B handle were web-confirmed by title/venue but verify the arXiv number renders before citing in committed code comments.

research.summary: The differential typology must be driven by the confidence interval on the DELTA between Claude's solve-rate and the cohort's solve-rate, not by comparing two separate intervals. Because Claude runs its own k attempts and the CohortSolver/panel runs a separate attempt set, this is an UNPAIRED two-proportion comparison at the puzzle-aggregate level — McNemar (already in stats.py) is the WRONG tool here; it only applies if the identical puzzle instances were attempted by both systems with trial-level matched pairing, which ai-crucible's architecture does not produce. The correct primary estimator is the Newcombe-Wilson hybrid interval for the difference of two independent proportions, which composes directly on the wilson_interval() already shipped. Miller's MDE math (2024) proves N=20/k=3 can only detect very large gaps, so a fourth class — "inconclusive/underpowered" — must be a first-class output, not a forced 3-way call. The popular "non-overlapping intervals" heuristic is statistically wrong (over-conservative; Schenker & Gentleman 2001) and must be rejected in favor of the difference-CI.

findings.claim: Comparing two models on the SAME questions should use paired differences, which gives a free variance reduction; but pairing requires the same items scored by both systems. ai-crucible's Claude attempts and CohortSolver attempts are different models on different attempt sets, so at the trial level they are NOT paired — the puzzle-aggregate solve-rates are two independent proportions, making this an UNPAIRED comparison and McNemar inapplicable.
findings.paper_title: Adding Error Bars to Evals: A Statistical Approach to Language Model Evaluations
findings.key_finding: Paired analysis reduces estimator variance by ~one-third (Var_paired = Var_unpaired - 2*Cov(x_A,x_B)/n) but ONLY when the same items are scored by both systems; absent that pairing the comparison is unpaired.
findings.design_implication: Make the primary classifier UNPAIRED: compute delta = claude_rate - cohort_rate from two independent Wilson proportions. Do NOT route the Claude-vs-cohort delta through mcnemar_exact() — reserve McNemar strictly for genuinely paired runs (same puzzle instances, both systems, matched attempts). Add a `paired: bool` flag to the classifier; when paired data IS available (e.g. two configs on the identical attempt log), prefer McNemar for the tighter test.

findings.claim: The minimum detectable effect shrinks only as 1/sqrt(N). At N=20 with k=3 the detectable delta is enormous, so most puzzles will have a delta whose CI straddles zero — forcing a 3-way call manufactures false gaps.
findings.paper_title: Adding Error Bars to Evals: A Statistical Approach to Language Model Evaluations
findings.key_finding: MDE delta = (z_alpha/2 + z_beta) * sqrt(variance/n); detecting a 3% difference needs ~969 independent questions at 80% power / 5% significance — so tiny N detects only very large gaps.
findings.design_implication: Introduce a FOURTH typology class INCONCLUSIVE_UNDERPOWERED as a first-class return value. A puzzle is only classified as a Claude-specific gap / LLM-general / Claude-strength when the Newcombe-Wilson 95% delta-CI EXCLUDES zero AND |delta| exceeds a minimum meaningful effect (default mde_floor=0.30 at N=20/k=3, configurable, derived from the MDE formula). Otherwise return inconclusive. Surface the realized MDE in the result so reviewers see why a puzzle is underpowered.

findings.claim: The confidence interval for the DIFFERENCE of two independent proportions, built from the two Wilson intervals, is the accurate small-N way to express a confident per-item delta — it composes directly on the Wilson interval already in stats.py.
findings.paper_title: Interval Estimation for the Difference Between Independent Proportions: Comparison of Eleven Methods
findings.key_finding: Newcombe's method 10 (hybrid score): for Wilson intervals (l1,u1) and (l2,u2), the delta CI is [d - sqrt((p1-l1)^2+(u2-p2)^2), d + sqrt((u1-p1)^2+(p2-l2)^2)] with d=p1-p2; it performs well at small N and near 0/1, beating chi-square methods.
findings.design_implication: Add newcombe_wilson_diff(s1,n1,s2,n2,conf=0.95) -> (lower,upper,delta) to scoring/stats.py, implemented by calling the existing wilson_interval() twice and combining per the formula. The classifier decides direction by the sign of the delta-CI when it excludes zero: lower>0 => Claude solves MORE; upper<0 => Claude solves LESS. This reuses the load-bearing Wilson estimator (no new dependency, DECOMPOSE_BY_SECRETS clean).

findings.claim: Judging significance by whether two separate confidence intervals overlap is a statistical error: overlapping intervals can still hide a significant difference. The correct test is whether the CI on the DIFFERENCE contains zero.
findings.paper_title: On Judging the Significance of Differences by Examining the Overlap Between Confidence Intervals
findings.key_finding: The interval-overlap method is systematically over-conservative (it misses real differences); two 95% intervals can overlap while the difference is significant at p<0.05, and non-overlap corresponds to a much stricter ~99.4% test.
findings.design_implication: Explicitly REJECT the 'non-overlapping Wilson intervals' heuristic named in the question as the classification rule. Classify on the Newcombe-Wilson delta-CI excluding zero instead. Document this in the function docstring so a future maintainer does not 'simplify' it back to interval-overlap and silently lose power.

findings.claim: When few independent runs are available (k~3), a paired bootstrap with a conservative decision rule refuses to declare significance for small improvements — exactly the guardrail ai-crucible wants against manufacturing gaps from noise.
findings.paper_title: When +1% Is Not Enough: A Paired Bootstrap Protocol for Evaluating Small Improvements
findings.key_finding: With only three seeds the paired BCa-bootstrap + sign-flip permutation protocol NEVER declares significance for 0.5-2 point gains, where naive single-run/unpaired-t-test analyses falsely do; conservatism is an intentional guardrail against over-claiming at low run counts.
findings.design_implication: Adopt the conservative-by-default posture: at the k=3 floor the classifier should overwhelmingly return INCONCLUSIVE for sub-MDE deltas, and this is correct behavior, not a bug. For puzzles that reach larger N, offer an optional paired-bootstrap path (resample puzzle-level paired deltas, 1000+ replicates, classify only if the 95% percentile interval excludes zero) when truly paired data exists; otherwise the Newcombe-Wilson analytic CI is the primary rule.

findings.claim: Across a whole catalog of puzzles each tested for a Claude-vs-cohort delta, the per-puzzle p-values must be corrected for multiple comparisons or the catalog will fill with false gaps; BH-FDR (already in stats.py) is the right correction because we want to control the proportion of false discoveries among flagged gaps, not familywise error.
findings.paper_title: Controlling the False Discovery Rate: A Practical and Powerful Approach to Multiple Testing
findings.key_finding: BH controls E[V/R] (expected fraction of false rejections among rejections) and is strictly more powerful than Bonferroni, appropriate when an overall family conclusion is not driven by any single inference.
findings.design_implication: Add a catalog-level classify_catalog(...) wrapper that derives a per-puzzle two-sided p-value for the delta (exact binomial via the discordant/aggregate counts, or the McNemar p when paired), runs the existing BH-FDR at q=0.10, and only assigns a directional typology class to puzzles that BOTH pass the per-puzzle Newcombe-Wilson delta-CI test AND survive BH-FDR. Non-survivors collapse to INCONCLUSIVE_UNDERPOWERED. This stops small-N noise from manufacturing false gaps en masse.

findings.claim: Clustered structure (multiple attempts per puzzle, multiple puzzles per family) inflates true standard errors far beyond the naive binomial, so per-attempt counts overstate precision; the unit of analysis for the delta must be the puzzle, not the pooled attempt.
findings.paper_title: Adding Error Bars to Evals: A Statistical Approach to Language Model Evaluations
findings.key_finding: Clustered standard errors can be over 3x larger than naive SEs on the same data; ignoring clustering makes an eval look far more precise than it is.
findings.design_implication: Compute each system's proportion at the puzzle level (successes out of N=20 attempts is one cluster) and treat the per-puzzle delta as the atomic unit; do NOT pool attempts across puzzles into one giant proportion. When aggregating a typology verdict across a family, cluster by puzzle. This keeps the Wilson and Newcombe-Wilson inputs honest and prevents the 3x precision overstatement.

research.recommendation:
Implement an UNPAIRED, delta-CI-driven classifier with a first-class inconclusive guard. Concrete signature for scoring/differential.py (composing the already-shipped wilson_interval):

  def newcombe_wilson_diff(s1: int, n1: int, s2: int, n2: int, conf: float = 0.95) -> tuple[float, float, float]:
      '''Newcombe(1998) hybrid-score CI for p1 - p2. Returns (lower, upper, delta).
      Built from two wilson_interval() calls: l/u from each, then
      lower = d - sqrt((p1-l1)^2 + (u2-p2)^2), upper = d + sqrt((u1-p1)^2 + (p2-l2)^2).'''

  class Typology(str, Enum):
      CLAUDE_SPECIFIC_GAP   # claude solves LESS than cohort, delta-CI excludes 0, upper<0
      CLAUDE_STRENGTH       # claude solves MORE, delta-CI excludes 0, lower>0
      LLM_GENERAL_GAP       # both rates low (e.g. both upper Wilson <= some floor), delta-CI contains 0
      INCONCLUSIVE_UNDERPOWERED  # delta-CI contains 0 OR |delta| < mde_floor and not jointly-low

  def classify_puzzle(claude_succ:int, claude_n:int, cohort_succ:int, cohort_n:int, *, conf=0.95, mde_floor=0.30, general_fail_ceiling=0.20, paired:bool=False, mcnemar_b:int|None=None, mcnemar_c:int|None=None) -> ClassifyResult

Decision order (the inconclusive guard is FIRST-class, evaluated explicitly):
  1. Compute claude Wilson, cohort Wilson, and newcombe_wilson_diff.
  2. SIGNIFICANT-DIRECTIONAL: if the delta-CI excludes zero AND |delta| >= mde_floor:
       upper < 0  -> CLAUDE_SPECIFIC_GAP (Claude underperforms cohort — the highest-value class)
       lower > 0  -> CLAUDE_STRENGTH (anti-regression)
  3. JOINT-FAIL (LLM-general frontier gap): if delta-CI CONTAINS zero AND BOTH Wilson uppers <= general_fail_ceiling (both systems demonstrably fail, not merely unmeasured) -> LLM_GENERAL_GAP. This requires positive evidence of shared failure, not just a null delta.
  4. Else -> INCONCLUSIVE_UNDERPOWERED, and the result carries the realized MDE (computed from the Miller formula at the observed N/k) so reviewers see the puzzle needs more N before any directional claim.

When paired=True and mcnemar_b/c are supplied (the SAME puzzle instances attempted by both systems with matched pairing), use mcnemar_exact(b,c) for the significance test in step 2 instead of the Newcombe delta-CI sign — McNemar is strictly more powerful on truly paired data and is already in stats.py. Default paired=False because ai-crucible's Claude and CohortSolver run independent attempt sets.

Catalog level: classify_catalog(results) computes a per-puzzle two-sided delta p-value (exact binomial on aggregate counts, or McNemar p when paired), applies the existing BH-FDR(q=0.10), and downgrades any puzzle that does NOT survive BH to INCONCLUSIVE_UNDERPOWERED even if its solo delta-CI excluded zero. Unit of analysis is the puzzle (one cluster of N attempts), never pooled attempts, per Miller's clustered-SE warning. Persist delta, delta_ci, realized_mde, p_value, bh_survived, and the class into the attestation event store so every classification is replayable (PIN_PER_STEP) and auditable.

Explicitly REJECT the non-overlapping-Wilson-intervals heuristic from the question (Schenker & Gentleman 2001: over-conservative, loses power) — classify on the delta-CI, with a docstring warning so it is not 'simplified' back.
research.open_risks: 1) mde_floor=0.30 and general_fail_ceiling=0.20 are defensible defaults derived from the MDE math at N=20/k=3 but are policy choices; they should be config-pinned per catalog and revisited if N grows. 2) The exact binomial p-value feeding BH-FDR for the unpaired case is an approximation of the Newcombe-Wilson test (the two do not produce identical p-values); for strict coherence one could derive the p-value by inverting the Newcombe interval, which is more code — acceptable to defer but should be noted in the docstring. 3) At the k=3 floor almost everything will land in INCONCLUSIVE_UNDERPOWERED; this is statistically correct but means the diagnostic payoff (Claude-specific gaps) only materializes for puzzles run at higher N or with very large true deltas — the catalog should expect to escalate promising-but-underpowered puzzles to more attempts rather than force a class. 4) LLM_GENERAL_GAP requires BOTH Wilson uppers below the fail ceiling; a puzzle where Claude clearly fails but the cohort is simply unmeasured (small cohort N) will correctly fall to inconclusive rather than be mislabeled general — but this means cohort N must be funded or many gaps stay inconclusive. 5) The paired path (McNemar) is only valid if the SAME puzzle instance and matched attempts are truly used by both systems; mislabeling independent runs as paired would understate variance — guard with an assertion that paired=True requires explicit b/c counts from a single shared attempt log.

research.summary: For a measurement instrument, the literature settles decisively on event-sourcing: an append-only run log is the authoritative source of truth and the tier-state catalog is a DERIVED projection rebuildable by replay — never a hand-maintained mutable table. ai-crucible already owns the right primitive (the SHA-256 hash-chained JsonlEventStore over RFC-8785 canonical JSON); Epic 4 should extend it, not invent a new store. Tier transitions (Lab→Arena, Arena→Regression) become first-class event types carrying reason + evidence-snapshot + timestamp, making them idempotent (replay reproduces identical state) and legible (each transition is its own auditable record with the exact Wilson counts that triggered it). Storage: keep JSON-lines as the durable backing (it is the audit artifact, content-hashed and diffable in git) and build an OPTIONAL stdlib sqlite3 projection only as a queryable cache rebuilt-from-log, never as the source of truth.

findings.claim: The reliability/eval store should be append-only events with current tier state as a derived projection (CQRS read model), not a mutable catalog row that gets overwritten. Audit and full reproducibility are intrinsic properties of this pattern: the event log is the authoritative source and state is reproduced by replay + hash verification.
findings.paper_title: Event Sourcing (pattern) — Martin Fowler; Event Sourcing Pattern — Azure Architecture Center
findings.authors: Martin Fowler (2005); Microsoft Azure Architecture Center
findings.key_finding: Every state change is stored as an immutable event in an append-only log and current state is a projection of those events, giving full audit and replay-based reproducibility for free.
findings.design_implication: Make the per-(puzzle,model) solve-history run log the authoritative store and derive tier state (Lab/Arena/Regression) by folding events. ai-crucible's JsonlEventStore is the write side; a catalog.json (or sqlite cache) is a rebuildable read-side projection. NEVER UPDATE a tier in place — append a transition event and re-fold.

findings.claim: Provenance must record what was done, by whom, with what inputs, in what order, cryptographically chained — the model ai-crucible already mirrors with prev_hash/hash. The catalog should carry an in-toto/SLSA-shaped provenance record per run (model id+version, prompt/tool-schema pin, puzzle content hash, oracle hash, panel.json hash).
findings.paper_title: in-toto: Providing farm-to-table guarantees for bits and bytes
findings.key_finding: Supply-chain integrity is achieved by cryptographically recording and verifying every step — what was performed, by whom, and in what order — from inception to artifact.
findings.design_implication: Each run event payload must pin the full provenance tuple (model_id, model_version, puzzle_content_hash, oracle_content_hash, panel_artifact_hash, prompt+tool-schema hash, k, n). This makes a pass^k figure attributable to an exact instrument configuration and replayable — the PIN_PER_STEP standard, enforced at the catalog boundary.

findings.claim: Provenance has graded levels; ai-crucible can declare an honest SLSA-style level for its catalog rather than overclaiming. The hash-chained JSONL alone is roughly L1 (documented, tamper-evident provenance); cosign/Rekor signing (the existing stub) would reach L2+ (authenticated, signed). The catalog should record its own provenance level honestly, matching the repo's no-fake-signature discipline.
findings.paper_title: SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts (specification)
findings.authors: Open Source Security Foundation (OpenSSF), originated at Google
findings.key_finding: SLSA's build track defines L0–L3: from no provenance (L0), to documented provenance (L1), to authenticated/signed provenance with identity (L2), to isolated/hardened builds (L3).
findings.design_implication: Stamp each catalog/transition record with a provenance_level field (1 when hash-chain-only, 2 when a cosign signature is attached). Honest leveling matches attestation.py's 'honest provenance or none, never plausible-looking' rule and lets a reader trust the catalog at exactly its real strength.

findings.claim: Tier transitions must be timestamped with a trusted, verifiable proof-of-existence so the capability-evolution timeline is defensible — not just a wall-clock string the writer could backdate. RFC 3161 timestamping over the transition's content hash is the standard mechanism and composes with the existing event hash.
findings.paper_title: RFC 3161 — Internet X.509 PKI Time-Stamp Protocol (TSP)
findings.key_finding: A Time-Stamp Authority binds a hash of a datum to a time, producing a signed token that proves the datum existed at or before that time.
findings.design_implication: Each tier-transition entry carries an ISO-8601 recorded_at PLUS the transition's content hash; the timeline's defensibility comes from the hash chain (order is cryptographically fixed) with an optional RFC-3161 token field for transitions that must survive an adversarial 'you backdated the graduation' challenge. Order is already tamper-evident via prev_hash; the timestamp token upgrades it to externally-anchored.

findings.claim: Living benchmarks key items by RELEASE DATE / version windows so a model is scored only on items it could not have memorized. The catalog must record per-puzzle authored_at/first_seen and per-run model_cutoff so contamination-aware slices are computable from the log forever.
findings.paper_title: LiveCodeBench: Holistic and Contamination Free Evaluation of LLMs for Code
findings.authors: Jain, Han, Gu, Li, Yan, Zhang, Wang, Solar-Lezama, Sen, Stoica
findings.key_finding: Annotating problems with release dates lets a model with cutoff D be evaluated only on items released after D, exposing contamination as a performance drop across the cutoff boundary.
findings.design_implication: Catalog puzzle records MUST store authored_at (puzzle first_seen) and run records MUST store the Solver's training_cutoff. The differential typology then gains a contamination axis: a Claude-passes-others-fail result on a pre-cutoff puzzle is suspect; on a post-cutoff puzzle it is a genuine strength. Date-stamping is non-negotiable for a durable instrument.

findings.claim: Each evaluated item should pin its own reproducible execution environment, versioned per task and updatable over time without rewriting history — the model for ai-crucible's per-puzzle sandbox/setup_script pinning in the catalog. The store must be designed to accrete new items monthly while old items stay frozen (demote-not-delete).
findings.paper_title: SWE-bench Goes Live!
findings.key_finding: A continuously-updating benchmark gives each task a dedicated, version-pinned Docker image for reproducible execution and accretes new dated tasks monthly while keeping prior tasks contamination-resistant.
findings.design_implication: The run event must pin the puzzle's environment fingerprint (sandbox image/digest if any, setup_script hash, staged bait set). Because tiers are demote-not-delete, a Regression puzzle keeps its original env pin forever; the catalog's accretion-friendly append-only shape is exactly right for monthly Lab additions without disturbing graduated items.

findings.claim: Benchmark artifacts need an embedded, immutable version/canary marker that travels with every record so leaked or copied data is detectable and records are unambiguously tied to a benchmark version. ai-crucible's schema_version + a per-catalog canary fit this directly.
findings.paper_title: Beyond the Imitation Game: Quantifying and extrapolating the capabilities of language models (BIG-bench)
findings.authors: Srivastava, Rastogi, Rao, et al. (BIG-bench collaboration)
findings.key_finding: Every task file carries an immutable canary string (and tasks are versioned) so benchmark data is traceable and its leakage into training corpora is detectable.
findings.design_implication: Stamp each catalog file and each puzzle record with schema_version (already the repo pattern in panel_store.py) and a stable per-puzzle canary/uuid. Refuse to load a newer major schema than understood (the existing load_panel forward-incompat guard) — version-pin the catalog the same way the panel artifact is pinned.

findings.claim: Run tracking organizes evidence around immutable 'runs' that log parameters, metrics, and output artifacts with metadata (start/end times) for later comparison and reproducibility — the schema template for ai-crucible's run record. The catalog should model a run as the atomic accumulation unit (one puzzle×model×k-trial batch).
findings.paper_title: Accelerating the Machine Learning Lifecycle with MLflow
findings.authors: Zaharia, Chen, Davidson, Ghodsi, Hong, Konwinski, Murching, et al.
findings.key_finding: MLflow organizes ML evidence around immutable runs that each record parameters, metrics, artifacts, and timing metadata for later visualization, comparison, and reproduction.
findings.design_implication: Define the catalog's atomic record as a RUN: {run_id, puzzle_id, model_id, model_version, k, n, successes, pass_k (bool), wilson_lower, wilson_upper, panel_solve_rate, started_at, finished_at, provenance pins}. Tier state is folded across runs; the run is what ai-crucible run appends. This is the well-trodden, tool-agnostic eval-run schema.

findings.claim: Content-addressable storage (hash of content, dedup identical content, lightweight pointer files) is the standard for versioning datasets reproducibly and linking a code/state version to the exact data version. ai-crucible should content-hash puzzle artifacts and reference them by hash from run records.
findings.paper_title: DVC — Data Version Control (documentation / system)
findings.key_finding: DVC versions data via content-addressable storage (content hashes), storing each unique content once and linking exact data versions to code via small pointer files for reproducible pipelines.
findings.design_implication: Reference puzzles by puzzle_content_hash (a content hash over meta.json + oracle + setup_script) rather than by mutable path/name. A run record points at the puzzle by hash, so a later edit to a puzzle yields a NEW content hash and a distinct lineage — the capability-evolution timeline never silently conflates two different puzzle versions under one id.

research.recommendation:
Build Epic 4's catalog as event-sourced on top of the EXISTING attestation.JsonlEventStore — do not introduce a mutable catalog table and do not add a new dependency. Concrete design:

(1) STORAGE FORMAT — JSON-lines is the source of truth; sqlite3 is an optional derived cache. The durable, audit-grade store is one append-only hash-chained JSONL log per repo (e.g. catalog/runs.jsonl) using the existing JsonlEventStore (RFC-8785 canonical JSON, prev_hash/hash chain, verify_hash_chain). It is diffable, git-committable, content-hashed, and externally verifiable with zero deps — the audit artifact itself. A queryable view (tier state, latest pass^k per puzzle×model) is a PROJECTION derived by folding the log; cache it as a generated catalog.json (the panel_store.py pattern: schema_version-stamped, regenerate-don't-edit, forward-incompat guard) and OPTIONALLY into an ephemeral stdlib sqlite3 file built by `ai-crucible catalog rebuild` for ad-hoc queries. The sqlite file is never authoritative and is .gitignored; if it disagrees with the log, the log wins.

(2) TWO EVENT TYPES (one log, discriminated by `event` field):
  RUN event payload — {event:"run", run_id, puzzle_id, puzzle_content_hash, model_id, model_version, model_training_cutoff, role, k, n, successes, pass_k:bool, wilson_lower, wilson_upper, panel_solve_rate, meets_quorum, env_fingerprint:{setup_script_hash, sandbox_digest|null, bait_files}, prompt_tool_schema_hash, panel_artifact_hash, started_at, finished_at, schema_version}.
  TRANSITION event payload — {event:"transition", transition_id, puzzle_id, from_tier, to_tier, reason_code (one of GRADUATE_WILSON | SATURATED_REGRESSION | DEMOTE_NOT_DELETE | MANUAL), evidence:{aggregate_successes, aggregate_n, wilson_lower, wilson_upper, contributing_run_ids[]}, decided_by ("graduates()" | model_id | "human:mike"), recorded_at, schema_version}. Transitions cite the exact run_ids and Wilson counts that triggered them — legible and re-derivable.

(3) IDEMPOTENCY — both event types carry a deterministic id: run_id = sha256(puzzle_content_hash + model_version + prompt_tool_schema_hash + batch_nonce); transition_id = sha256(puzzle_id + from_tier + to_tier + sorted(contributing_run_ids)). The folder skips an event whose id is already folded, so replaying the log is idempotent and a transition fires AT MOST ONCE for a given evidence set. Tier state is `derive_tiers(log)` = fold runs into per-puzzle aggregates, apply graduates()/saturation rule, emit transition only if the derived target tier differs from the current folded tier — so `ai-crucible run` appending a run that crosses the Wilson threshold deterministically yields exactly one transition event, never a duplicate.

(4) CONCURRENCY (rare, serial, one-human studio) — guard the single appender with an OS-level exclusive lock file (catalog/.lock via O_CREAT|O_EXCL, the named compensator being lock removal on clean exit) so two overlapping `ai-crucible run` invocations serialize. On lock contention, the second run blocks-then-appends (its events are still order-fixed by prev_hash). Because JsonlEventStore reads the tail to compute prev_hash, the lock is what prevents a torn interleave; verify_hash_chain is the post-hoc check that catches any lock failure. No multi-writer DB transactions needed — this is the correct weight for a serial studio.

(5) PROVENANCE LEVEL — stamp provenance_level=1 on records backed by hash-chain only; upgrade to 2 when attestation.cosign_sign_blob attaches a real signature. Optional RFC-3161 token field on transition events for externally-anchored timeline defensibility. Demote-not-delete is native: a REGRESSION transition is just another appended event; the puzzle's history (and its frozen env_fingerprint) is preserved forever, exactly as the tier model requires.
research.open_risks: Hash-chained JSONL is tamper-EVIDENT, not tamper-PROOF: a malicious single-writer who controls the file can rewrite the whole chain from a chosen point (every subsequent hash recomputes consistently). Only the cosign/Rekor signing (existing stub) or an RFC-3161 token makes a rewrite externally detectable — until that ships, the catalog's integrity guarantee is 'accidental corruption + naive tampering,' which should be stated honestly (SLSA L1, not L2). Second: the sqlite projection invites drift if anyone treats it as authoritative or hand-edits it; the design must make 'log wins, sqlite is a cache' a hard, tested invariant (a `catalog verify` that rebuilds-and-diffs), or the dual store becomes a liability. Third: idempotent transitions depend on a STABLE saturation/graduation rule — if graduates() thresholds change, replaying the historical log re-derives DIFFERENT transitions than were originally recorded; the catalog must pin the rule version per transition (rule_version field) so the timeline reflects the decision actually made at the time, not a retroactive re-judgment. Fourth: content-hashing puzzles by their full artifact means any trivial edit (a typo fix in meta.json) forks lineage; needs a documented policy on what is a 'new puzzle version' vs an in-place fix, or the timeline fragments. Fifth: log growth is unbounded (append-only forever) — fine at studio scale for years, but a periodic signed snapshot/checkpoint (fold state + chain-anchor hash) should be planned before the log reaches millions of lines so replay stays fast.

integrated_recommendations.decision: Lab->Arena promotion is a THREE-VALUED predicate returning {PROMOTE, HOLD, DEFER_TO_DESIGNER}, gating on BOTH a cross-family SOLVE clause and a cross-family JUDGE-FAIRNESS clause (they catch orthogonal failure modes: solve-rate is blind to broken/underspecified items; a fairness vote is blind to triviality). Clause 1 (unchanged): keep the existing fixed-N Wilson band on Claude's OWN rate via stats.graduates() (0.10<=wilson_lower AND wilson_upper<=0.90) -- fail => HOLD. Clause 2 (NEW, solve, non-trivial-for-others): wilson_upper(cohort_succ, cohort_n) <= 0.90 -- if the cross-family cohort trivially aces it => HOLD. Clause 3 (NEW, judge-fairness, abstention-aware keystone): consume the panel signals that ALREADY EXIST -- if panel.meets_quorum is False (2<PoLL's 3) OR panel escalate margin is thin, the fairness verdict is LOW-CONFIDENCE => DEFER_TO_DESIGNER (the Trust-or-Escalate path); a confident 'unfair/broken' => HOLD; confident 'fair' => fairness_ok. PROMOTE iff claude_band_ok AND cohort_nontrivial AND fairness_ok AND not deferred. Promotion out of DEFER requires an explicit attested Designer gold-check override; never auto-promote on a deferred fairness verdict, never synthesize a fake quorum. Use fixed-N Wilson here (graduation is a one-shot decision at the pre-chosen N=20 floor) -- do NOT swap in the confidence-SEQUENCE estimator used for saturation. At graduation, call the differential classifier (see 'differential' area) to stamp the Arena entry with its born typology label, accepting INCONCLUSIVE_UNDERPOWERED as a valid born-state. Cohort must be run at the SAME N=20 floor or Clause 2's Wilson-upper rarely drops below 0.90 and everything looks non-trivial.
integrated_recommendations.predicate_or_signature: promote_decision(claude_successes:int, claude_n:int, cohort_successes:int, cohort_n:int, panel) -> Decision  where Decision = Enum(PROMOTE, HOLD, DEFER_TO_DESIGNER). claude_band_ok = stats.graduates(claude_successes, claude_n); cohort_nontrivial = stats.wilson_interval(cohort_successes, cohort_n).upper <= 0.90; deferred = (not panel.meets_quorum) or panel.escalate; PROMOTE iff claude_band_ok and cohort_nontrivial and (panel fairness confident-fair) and not deferred; emit graduation receipt {covered, held, deferred, realized_risk}; on DEFER append graduation_deferred event (panel margin, meets_quorum=false, seat ids, born differential class) to attestation store, blocking auto-promote until graduation_override.
integrated_recommendations.grounding: SWE-bench Verified (OpenAI/Chowdhury et al. 2024, openai.com/index/introducing-swe-bench-verified) WEB-CONFIRMED: 38.3% underspecified + 61.1% unfair unit tests, 68.3% filtered to 500 -- proves judge-fairness catches a mode solve-rate cannot see. Trust or Escalate (Jung, Brahman, Choi, arXiv:2407.18370, ICLR 2025) WEB-CONFIRMED: cascaded selective evaluation provably guarantees user-specified human agreement by abstaining when not confident -- the exact license for DEFER on sub-quorum. PoLL (Verga et al. 2024, arXiv:2404.18796) WEB-CONFIRMED: disjoint-family juries reduce intra-model bias -- justifies requiring cross-family at all and treating 2<3 families as genuinely weaker => deferral default.

integrated_recommendations.decision: Saturation is NOT wilson_upper>0.90 on a single batch (it flaps). Implement an ANTI-FLAPPING per-(puzzle, model) demotion predicate with a Schmitt-trigger HYSTERESIS BAND + a DWELL condition + a FRONTIER GATE, aggregated to a per-puzzle Arena status. DEMOTE Arena->Regression for (puzzle, model m) iff ALL of: (1) HIGH BAR: a time-uniform LOWER confidence bound on m's solve rate >= 0.90 -- use a confidence SEQUENCE (anytime-valid), NOT fixed-N wilson_lower, because the catalog peeks every run and acting on a fixed-N interval at a data-dependent stopping time inflates the false-demotion rate; (2) DWELL: that signal holds across >= dwell_K consecutive independent RUNS (default 2, per-puzzle configurable) AND accumulated post-trigger attempts >= 3*min_k ('integrate before you signal'); (3) FRONTIER GATE: m is a current-frontier / panel-admitted capable subject -- a weak local model passing never demotes, and everyone-fails stays in Arena (it is the LLM-general frontier gap, maximally diagnostic). RE-PROMOTE Regression->Arena iff the time-uniform UPPER bound on the best current subject's rate <= 0.75; the 0.75..0.90 dead band is the guard band that kills demote/re-promote oscillation -- wire the re-promotion path, not just demotion (a genuine new-version capability REGRESSION must be able to re-promote). Cleanest pure-Python implementation matching repo style: one accumulating per-(puzzle,model) E-PROCESS for H0:p<=0.90 vs H1:p>=0.97 built on the beta-binomial machinery already in scoring/stats.py; each run multiplies in the run's likelihood factor and appends to the hash-chained store; demote when the e-process crosses 1/alpha=20 AND the frontier gate holds -- making dwell EMERGENT (cannot cross on one lucky run) rather than a magic constant. Treat each k-attempt RUN (not each attempt) as the e-process unit and require dwell across SEPARATE runs/seeds/dates to respect Liu-2025 within-batch failure correlation. Record WHICH model + date saturated each puzzle (capability-evolution timeline); demote-not-delete.
integrated_recommendations.predicate_or_signature: saturation_eprocess(puzzle_id, model_id) -> float (running E-value, append per run). demote_decision(puzzle_id, model_id, runs) -> bool : cs_lower = confidence_sequence_lower(successes, n, alpha=0.05) ; demote iff e_value >= 20 AND cs_lower >= 0.90 AND dwell_runs >= dwell_K AND post_trigger_n >= 3*min_k AND is_frontier(model_id). repromote_decision(...) -> bool : confidence_sequence_upper(best_subject) <= 0.75. dwell_K and min_k per-puzzle configurable; alpha (=> threshold 20), H1=0.97, and the 0.75/0.90 band are rule-version-pinned policy knobs, NOT derived constants.
integrated_recommendations.grounding: Page CUSUM (Biometrika 1954, doi:10.1093/biomet/41.1-2.100) RECALLED -- integrate evidence before signaling, lower detection delay than per-batch Shewhart. Howard, Ramdas, McAuliffe, Sekhon time-uniform confidence sequences (Annals of Statistics 2021, 49(2):1055-1080, doi:10.1214/20-AOS1991; arXiv:1810.08240) WEB-CONFIRMED -- valid coverage at EVERY sample count, the rigorous fix for 'I checked the interval after each batch'. When AI Benchmarks Plateau (Akhtar, Reuel et al., arXiv:2602.16763, Feb 2026, 60 benchmarks) WEB-CONFIRMED -- saturation = loss of reliable discriminative power; act only when an uncertainty-aware saturation index CONSISTENTLY exceeds high thresholds; expert-curated benchmarks resist saturation, hiding test data gives no protection.

integrated_recommendations.decision: Drive the typology from the confidence interval on the DELTA between Claude's solve-rate and the cohort's solve-rate -- NOT by comparing two separate intervals (the non-overlapping-Wilson-intervals heuristic is statistically wrong: over-conservative, loses power -- explicitly REJECT it with a docstring warning). Because Claude and the CohortSolver run independent attempt sets, this is an UNPAIRED two-proportion comparison: McNemar (already in stats.py) is the WRONG tool here -- reserve it strictly for genuinely paired runs (same puzzle instances, matched attempts on one shared attempt log; gate behind paired=True requiring explicit b/c counts). Primary estimator: the Newcombe-1998 hybrid-score CI for p1-p2, built from two existing wilson_interval() calls. Add a FOURTH class INCONCLUSIVE_UNDERPOWERED as a first-class return (NOT a forced 3-way): at N=20/k=3 the MDE is large, so most puzzles legitimately land here -- that is correct behavior, not a bug. Classify directionally ONLY when the delta-CI EXCLUDES zero AND |delta| >= mde_floor (default 0.30, config-pinned, derived from Miller's MDE math at N=20/k=3): upper<0 => CLAUDE_SPECIFIC_GAP (Claude underperforms cohort, highest-value); lower>0 => CLAUDE_STRENGTH (anti-regression). LLM_GENERAL_GAP requires POSITIVE shared-failure evidence (delta-CI contains 0 AND both Wilson uppers <= general_fail_ceiling, default 0.20), never merely a null delta. Unit of analysis is the PUZZLE (one cluster of N attempts), never pooled attempts (clustered SEs run >3x naive). Catalog level: classify_catalog() applies the EXISTING BH-FDR (q=0.10) across per-puzzle delta p-values and downgrades non-survivors to INCONCLUSIVE_UNDERPOWERED even if their solo delta-CI excluded zero. This same classifier is invoked at graduation time to stamp each Arena entry's born label (one measurement, two uses).
integrated_recommendations.predicate_or_signature: newcombe_wilson_diff(s1:int,n1:int,s2:int,n2:int,conf=0.95)->tuple[lower,upper,delta]  (two wilson_interval() calls then lower=d-sqrt((p1-l1)^2+(u2-p2)^2), upper=d+sqrt((u1-p1)^2+(p2-l2)^2)). classify_puzzle(claude_succ,claude_n,cohort_succ,cohort_n,*,conf=0.95,mde_floor=0.30,general_fail_ceiling=0.20,paired=False,mcnemar_b=None,mcnemar_c=None)->ClassifyResult{class, delta, delta_ci, realized_mde, p_value, bh_survived}. Typology=Enum(CLAUDE_SPECIFIC_GAP, CLAUDE_STRENGTH, LLM_GENERAL_GAP, INCONCLUSIVE_UNDERPOWERED). classify_catalog(results,q=0.10) runs stats BH-FDR; persist all fields to attestation store (PIN_PER_STEP).
integrated_recommendations.grounding: Newcombe 1998 'Interval estimation for the difference between independent proportions: comparison of eleven methods' (Statistics in Medicine 17(8):873-890, doi:10.1002/(SICI)1097-0258(19980430)17:8<873::AID-SIM779>3.0.CO;2-I) WEB-CONFIRMED -- the Wilson-combining hybrid 'performs well and is readily implemented irrespective of sample size'. Schenker & Gentleman 2001 (The American Statistician 55(3):182-186, doi:10.1198/000313001317097960) WEB-CONFIRMED -- interval-overlap is over-conservative and fails to reject more often when it should; test the difference instead. Evan Miller 'Adding Error Bars to Evals' (arXiv:2411.00640, 2024) WEB-CONFIRMED (paper/scope; specific 969-question MDE and 3x clustered-SE figures recalled from the PDF body) -- MDE ~ 1/sqrt(N) so tiny N detects only large gaps; cluster by puzzle.

integrated_recommendations.decision: Build the catalog as EVENT-SOURCED on top of the EXISTING attestation.JsonlEventStore -- do not invent a new store, do not add a dependency, do not maintain a mutable tier table. JSON-lines hash-chained log (RFC-8785 canonical JSON, prev_hash/hash, verify_hash_chain) is the durable, git-diffable, externally-verifiable SOURCE OF TRUTH (the audit artifact itself). Tier state (Lab/Arena/Regression) is a DERIVED projection rebuilt by folding events -- NEVER update a tier in place; append a transition event and re-fold. An optional stdlib sqlite3 view is a rebuildable cache only (.gitignored, 'log wins' as a hard tested invariant via 'catalog verify' that rebuilds-and-diffs); it is never authoritative. Two event types in one log: RUN {run_id, puzzle_id, puzzle_content_hash, model_id, model_version, model_training_cutoff, role, k, n, successes, pass_k:bool, wilson_lower, wilson_upper, panel_solve_rate, meets_quorum, env_fingerprint, prompt_tool_schema_hash, panel_artifact_hash, started_at, finished_at, schema_version, rule_version} and TRANSITION {transition_id, puzzle_id, from_tier, to_tier, reason_code in [GRADUATE_WILSON|DEFER_TO_DESIGNER|SATURATED_REGRESSION|DEMOTE_NOT_DELETE|MANUAL], evidence{aggregate_successes, aggregate_n, wilson bounds, contributing_run_ids}, decided_by, recorded_at, schema_version, rule_version}. IDEMPOTENCY via deterministic ids: run_id=sha256(puzzle_content_hash+model_version+prompt_tool_schema_hash+batch_nonce), transition_id=sha256(puzzle_id+from_tier+to_tier+sorted(contributing_run_ids)); the folder skips already-folded ids so replay is idempotent and a transition fires at most once per evidence set. CRITICAL cross-cutting fix: PIN rule_version on every run AND transition -- because the graduation/saturation thresholds are policy knobs, replaying the log under changed thresholds would otherwise re-derive DIFFERENT transitions than were actually decided; rule_version makes the timeline reflect the decision made AT THE TIME, not a retroactive re-judgment. Reference puzzles by puzzle_content_hash (hash over meta.json + oracle + setup_script) so an edit forks a NEW lineage and the timeline never silently conflates two puzzle versions; store authored_at/first_seen per puzzle and model_training_cutoff per run for a contamination axis. Stamp provenance_level honestly (1=hash-chain-only, 2=cosign-signed) -- never overclaim; serialize the single appender with an O_CREAT|O_EXCL lock file (named compensator: lock removal on clean exit) since this is a serial one-human studio.
integrated_recommendations.predicate_or_signature: Reuse attestation.JsonlEventStore (append, verify_hash_chain). derive_tiers(log)->dict[puzzle_id, tier] : fold RUN events into per-(puzzle,model) aggregates, apply promote_decision()/demote_decision() pinned at each event's rule_version, emit a TRANSITION only when derived target tier != current folded tier. catalog_rebuild() regenerates catalog.json (schema_version-stamped, forward-incompat guard a la panel_store.py) + optional sqlite cache. catalog_verify() rebuilds from log and diffs the cache (log wins). run_id / transition_id as sha256 above for at-most-once idempotent transitions.
integrated_recommendations.grounding: Event Sourcing (Fowler 2005, martinfowler.com/eaaDev/EventSourcing.html; Azure Architecture Center) RECALLED -- append-only events are authoritative, current state is a replay projection => audit + reproducibility for free. in-toto (Torres-Arias et al., USENIX Security 2019) RECALLED + SLSA spec (OpenSSF, slsa.dev) RECALLED -- pin the provenance tuple per run and declare an HONEST level (hash-chain-only ~ L1, signed ~ L2). LiveCodeBench (Jain et al., arXiv:2403.07974, 2024) RECALLED -- date-stamp puzzles (authored_at) and record model training_cutoff so contamination-aware slices are computable forever; DVC content-addressable storage (iterative.ai) RECALLED -- reference puzzles by content hash so edits fork lineage.

synthesis.cross_cutting_risks:
CROSS-CUTTING RISKS / CONTRADICTIONS BETWEEN THE FOUR ANSWERS:

1. SHARED COHORT-N FLOOR (Q1+Q2+Q3 all silently depend on it). Q1's Clause 2 Wilson-upper, Q2's frontier gate, and Q3's Newcombe delta-CI all degrade or become meaningless if the cross-family cohort is run at a smaller N than Claude. LOCK: the cohort runs at the SAME N=20 floor as Claude. Without this, Clause 2 rarely fires (everything looks 'non-trivial'), the delta-CI is too wide to ever exclude zero (everything is INCONCLUSIVE), and 'is this model frontier-capable' is undercounted. This is the single most load-bearing consistency requirement across the swarm and is currently only stated as an open-risk in Q1/Q3, never locked.

2. TWO DIFFERENT WILSON-FAMILY ESTIMATORS, DO NOT UNIFY (Q1 vs Q2). Graduation (Q1) correctly uses FIXED-N Wilson -- it is a one-shot decision at a pre-chosen N. Saturation (Q2) correctly uses a confidence SEQUENCE / e-process -- it peeks every run, so fixed-N intervals would inflate the false-demotion rate. A maintainer who 'simplifies' both to call wilson_interval() would silently break saturation's anytime-validity. The lock keeps them distinct on purpose; flag loudly in code comments.

3. REPLAY DETERMINISM vs MUTABLE POLICY KNOBS (Q2 thresholds vs Q4 idempotency). Q2 declares H1=0.97, the 0.75/0.90 band, dwell_K, mde_floor as policy knobs; Q4's idempotent-transition guarantee assumes a STABLE rule -- if graduates()/saturation thresholds change, replaying the historical log re-derives DIFFERENT transitions. RESOLUTION (locked): pin rule_version on every run AND transition event; derive_tiers() evaluates each event under ITS OWN rule_version, so the timeline reflects the decision actually made, not a retroactive re-judgment. This is the keystone that makes Q2's tunability and Q4's event-sourcing coexist.

4. SELF-PREFERENCE LEAK ON THE FAIRNESS AXIS (Q1 keystone vs EXTERNAL_VERIFIER standard). Routing DEFER_TO_DESIGNER to the Claude Designer reintroduces a SAME-FAMILY judge on the fairness axis -- the exact self-preference the EXTERNAL_VERIFIER workflow standard and PoLL's cross-family rationale guard against. Acceptable ONLY as the disclosed, attested escalation of last resort under structural sub-quorum (omega on ice, no 3rd disjoint family staffable). MITIGATION (must be wired): monitor the DEFER fraction as an instrument-health metric; a RISING defer rate means recruit a third disjoint family, NOT relax the threshold. A silently high defer rate quietly transfers the instrument's fairness authority back onto Claude -- the failure mode the whole cross-family design exists to prevent.

5. CALIBRATION GAP IN THE ABSTENTION THRESHOLD (Q1 over-reach check). Trust-or-Escalate's PROVABLE human-agreement guarantee assumes a CALIBRATED confidence signal (their Simulated Annotators). A raw reliability_weighted_vote margin from a 2-seat panel is NOT a calibrated human-agreement probability. So Q1's DEFER thresholds chosen by margin are HEURISTIC, not provably bounded -- the design borrows Trust-or-Escalate's POSTURE (abstain-and-escalate) legitimately, but must NOT claim its provable guarantee. The conformal/coverage receipt reports the spread but does not manufacture calibration. State this honestly; do not market the gate as 'provably bounded false-graduation risk'.

6. INDEPENDENCE ASSUMPTION vs LIU-2025 CORRELATED FAILURES (Q2). The e-process/SPRT error guarantees assume runs i.i.d. at the puzzle's true p; Liu-2025's 71% identical-failure recurrence violates this WITHIN a batch. Mitigation (locked into Q2): the e-process unit is the k-attempt RUN, not the attempt, and dwell is required across SEPARATE runs (different seeds/dates) -- never let within-batch correlation masquerade as accumulated cross-run evidence.

7. TAMPER-EVIDENT != TAMPER-PROOF (Q4 honesty). The hash-chained JSONL is tamper-EVIDENT only; a malicious single-writer can rewrite the whole chain consistently. Until cosign/Rekor signing or an RFC-3161 token ships, state the integrity guarantee honestly as SLSA ~L1 ('accidental corruption + naive tampering'), matching attestation.py's 'honest provenance or none' rule. Do not stamp provenance_level=2 without a real signature.

8. MCNEMAR MISUSE GUARD (Q3, repo already ships mcnemar in stats.py). Because McNemar is present and tempting, there is a real risk a maintainer routes the unpaired Claude-vs-cohort delta through it, understating variance. The classifier must default paired=False and assert that paired=True requires explicit b/c counts from a single shared attempt log -- the architecture does not produce trial-level pairing between Claude and the CohortSolver.
